Assignment #2: Chapter 3¶

Christopher Lockard (mpl537)¶

June 21, 2026¶

2. Carefully explain the differences between the KNN classifier and KNN regression methods.¶

KNN classification and KNN regression both use the K nearest observations to make a prediction. The difference is the type of outcome they predict. KNN classification is used when the outcome is categorical, such as predicting whether a student is “retained” or “not retained.” It uses majority vote among the nearest neighbors. KNN regression is used when the outcome is numerical, such as predicting a student’s GPA. It uses the average value of the nearest neighbors. Therefore, classification predicts a category, while regression predicts a number.

9. This question involves the use of multiple linear regression on the Auto data set.¶

In [5]:
#(a) Produce a scatterplot matrix which includes all of the variables in the data set.
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from ISLP import load_data

Auto = load_data("Auto")
Auto.head()

pd.plotting.scatter_matrix(Auto, figsize=(12, 12))
plt.show()

The scatterplot matrix shows relationships among all of the numeric variables in the Auto data set. There appears to be a strong negative relationship between mpg and variables such as weight, horsepower, and displacement. This means that heavier cars and cars with more horsepower or displacement tend to have lower miles per gallon.

In [7]:
#(b) Compute the matrix of correlations between the variables using the DataFrame.corr() method.

Auto.corr()
Out[7]:
mpg cylinders displacement horsepower weight acceleration year origin
mpg 1.000000 -0.777618 -0.805127 -0.778427 -0.832244 0.423329 0.580541 0.565209
cylinders -0.777618 1.000000 0.950823 0.842983 0.897527 -0.504683 -0.345647 -0.568932
displacement -0.805127 0.950823 1.000000 0.897257 0.932994 -0.543800 -0.369855 -0.614535
horsepower -0.778427 0.842983 0.897257 1.000000 0.864538 -0.689196 -0.416361 -0.455171
weight -0.832244 0.897527 0.932994 0.864538 1.000000 -0.416839 -0.309120 -0.585005
acceleration 0.423329 -0.504683 -0.543800 -0.689196 -0.416839 1.000000 0.290316 0.212746
year 0.580541 -0.345647 -0.369855 -0.416361 -0.309120 0.290316 1.000000 0.181528
origin 0.565209 -0.568932 -0.614535 -0.455171 -0.585005 0.212746 0.181528 1.000000

The correlation matrix shows that mpg has strong negative correlations with weight (-0.832), displacement (-0.805), horsepower (-0.778), and cylinders (-0.778). This means that the more heavier and powerful cars will usually have lower mpg. However, mpg has positive correlations with year (0.581), origin (0.565), and acceleration (0.423). This means that newer cars of certain origins and with higher acceleration values tend to be associated with a higher mpg.

In [9]:
#(c) Use the sm.OLS() function to perform a multiple linear regression
#with mpg as the response and all other variables except name as
#the predictors. Use the summarize() function to print the results.

import statsmodels.api as sm
from ISLP.models import ModelSpec as MS

X = MS(Auto.columns.drop('mpg')).fit_transform(Auto)
y = Auto['mpg']

model = sm.OLS(y, X)
results = model.fit()

results.summary()
Out[9]:
OLS Regression Results
Dep. Variable: mpg R-squared: 0.821
Model: OLS Adj. R-squared: 0.818
Method: Least Squares F-statistic: 252.4
Date: Sun, 21 Jun 2026 Prob (F-statistic): 2.04e-139
Time: 14:06:20 Log-Likelihood: -1023.5
No. Observations: 392 AIC: 2063.
Df Residuals: 384 BIC: 2095.
Df Model: 7
Covariance Type: nonrobust
coef std err t P>|t| [0.025 0.975]
intercept -17.2184 4.644 -3.707 0.000 -26.350 -8.087
cylinders -0.4934 0.323 -1.526 0.128 -1.129 0.142
displacement 0.0199 0.008 2.647 0.008 0.005 0.035
horsepower -0.0170 0.014 -1.230 0.220 -0.044 0.010
weight -0.0065 0.001 -9.929 0.000 -0.008 -0.005
acceleration 0.0806 0.099 0.815 0.415 -0.114 0.275
year 0.7508 0.051 14.729 0.000 0.651 0.851
origin 1.4261 0.278 5.127 0.000 0.879 1.973
Omnibus: 31.906 Durbin-Watson: 1.309
Prob(Omnibus): 0.000 Jarque-Bera (JB): 53.100
Skew: 0.529 Prob(JB): 2.95e-12
Kurtosis: 4.460 Cond. No. 8.59e+04


Notes:
[1] Standard Errors assume that the covariance matrix of the errors is correctly specified.
[2] The condition number is large, 8.59e+04. This might indicate that there are
strong multicollinearity or other numerical problems.

i. Yes, there appears to be a relationship between the predictors and mpg. The overall F-statistic is 252.4 with a very small p-value, 2.04e-139. Since this p-value is much smaller than 0.05, we reject the null hypothesis and conclude that at least one predictor is related to mpg.

ii. The predictors that appear statistically significant are displacement, weight, year, and origin, because their p-values are below 0.05. The variables cylinders, horsepower, and acceleration do not appear statistically significant in this model because their p-values are above 0.05.

iii. The coefficient for year is 0.7508. This means that, holding the other predictors constant, a one-year increase is associated with an increase of about 0.75 miles per gallon. In other words, newer cars tend to have better gas mileage.

In [10]:
#(d)  Produce some of diagnostic plots of the linear regression fit as
#described in the lab. Comment on any problems you see with the
#fit. Do the residual plots suggest any unusually large outliers?
#Does the leverage plot identify any observations with unusually
#high leverage?

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import statsmodels.api as sm

# Residuals vs fitted values
plt.scatter(results.fittedvalues, results.resid)
plt.axhline(0)
plt.xlabel("Fitted values")
plt.ylabel("Residuals")
plt.title("Residuals vs Fitted")
plt.show()
In [11]:
sm.qqplot(results.resid, line="45")
plt.title("Normal Q-Q Plot")
plt.show()
In [12]:
influence = results.get_influence()
leverage = influence.hat_matrix_diag
student_resid = influence.resid_studentized_internal

plt.scatter(leverage, student_resid)
plt.axhline(0)
plt.xlabel("Leverage")
plt.ylabel("Studentized residuals")
plt.title("Studentized Residuals vs Leverage")
plt.show()

The residuals vs fitted plot shows a curved pattern rather than a completely random spread, which suggests that there may be some nonlinearity in the data. The Q-Q plot shows that the residuals do not perfectly follow the straight line, especially at the ends, which suggests possible non-normality or outliers. The leverage plot shows one point with much higher leverage than the others, so there may be at least one influential observation.

In [13]:
# Fit some models with interactions as described in the lab. Do
# any interactions appear to be statistically significant?

import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

interaction_model = smf.ols(
    "mpg ~ (cylinders + displacement + horsepower + weight + acceleration + year + origin) ** 2",
    data=Auto
).fit()

interaction_model.summary()
Out[13]:

[1] Standard Errors assume that the covariance matrix of the errors is correctly specified.
[2] The condition number is large, 3.76e+08. This might indicate that there are
strong multicollinearity or other numerical problems.

Yes, some interaction terms appear to be statistically significant. In particular, displacement:year, acceleration:year, and acceleration:origin have p-values below 0.05. This suggests that the relationship between some predictors and mpg may depend on the value of another predictor. For example, the effect of acceleration on mpg may differ by model year or origin.

In [15]:
#(f) Try a few different transformations of the variables, such as
#log(X),
#√
#X, X2. Comment on your findings.
import numpy as np
transform_model = smf.ols(
    "mpg ~ np.log(horsepower) + np.log(weight) + np.sqrt(displacement) + acceleration + year + origin",
    data=Auto
).fit()

transform_model.summary()
Out[15]:

np.sqrt(displacement) 0.2576 0.146 1.767 0.078 -0.029 0.544

Notes:
[1] Standard Errors assume that the covariance matrix of the errors is correctly specified.
[2] The condition number is large, 5.94e+03. This might indicate that there are
strong multicollinearity or other numerical problems.

I tried a model using transformations of some predictors, including log(horsepower), log(weight), and sqrt(displacement). The transformed model had an R-squared value of 0.848, which is higher than the original model’s R-squared of 0.821. This suggests that transformations improved the model fit. However, the interaction model had a higher R-squared of 0.889, so the interaction model explained more variation in mpg than the transformed model.

In the transformed model, log(horsepower), log(weight), year, and origin were statistically significant because their p-values were below 0.05.

10. This question should be answered using the Carseats data set.¶

In [16]:
#(a)
Carseats = load_data("Carseats")
Carseats.head()
Out[16]:
Sales CompPrice Income Advertising Population Price ShelveLoc Age Education Urban US
0 9.50 138 73 11 276 120 Bad 42 17 Yes Yes
1 11.22 111 48 16 260 83 Good 65 10 Yes Yes
2 10.06 113 35 10 269 80 Medium 59 12 Yes Yes
3 7.40 117 100 4 466 97 Medium 55 14 Yes Yes
4 4.15 141 64 3 340 128 Bad 38 13 Yes No
In [17]:
model_10 = smf.ols(
    "Sales ~ Price + Urban + US",
    data=Carseats
).fit()

model_10.summary()
Out[17]:
OLS Regression Results
Dep. Variable: Sales R-squared: 0.239
Model: OLS Adj. R-squared: 0.234
Method: Least Squares F-statistic: 41.52
Date: Sun, 21 Jun 2026 Prob (F-statistic): 2.39e-23
Time: 14:56:53 Log-Likelihood: -927.66
No. Observations: 400 AIC: 1863.
Df Residuals: 396 BIC: 1879.
Df Model: 3
Covariance Type: nonrobust
coef std err t P>|t| [0.025 0.975]
Intercept 13.0435 0.651 20.036 0.000 11.764 14.323
Urban[T.Yes] -0.0219 0.272 -0.081 0.936 -0.556 0.512
US[T.Yes] 1.2006 0.259 4.635 0.000 0.691 1.710
Price -0.0545 0.005 -10.389 0.000 -0.065 -0.044
Omnibus: 0.676 Durbin-Watson: 1.912
Prob(Omnibus): 0.713 Jarque-Bera (JB): 0.758
Skew: 0.093 Prob(JB): 0.684
Kurtosis: 2.897 Cond. No. 628.


Notes:
[1] Standard Errors assume that the covariance matrix of the errors is correctly specified.

The intercept is 13.0435. This is the predicted sales when Price = 0, Urban = No, and US = No. This may not be realistic because a price of 0 does not make much practical sense.

The coefficient for Price is -0.0545. This means that, holding Urban and US constant, each 1-unit increase in price is associated with a decrease of about 0.0545 units in sales.

The coefficient for Urban[T.Yes] is -0.0219. This means that, holding price and US location constant, stores in urban locations are predicted to have sales about 0.0219 lower than stores in non-urban locations. This effect is very small.

The coefficient for US[T.Yes] is 1.2006. This means that, holding price and urban location constant, stores in the US are predicted to have sales about 1.2006 higher than stores outside the US.

(c)¶

Predicted sales start at 13.0435. Sales decrease as price increases. Urban stores are predicted to have slightly lower sales, and US stores are predicted to have higher sales.

(d)¶

Predicted sales start at 13.0435. Sales decrease as price increases. Urban stores are predicted to have slightly lower sales, and US stores are predicted to have higher sales.

In [18]:
#(e)
model_10_small = smf.ols(
    "Sales ~ Price + US",
    data=Carseats
).fit()

model_10_small.summary()
Out[18]:
OLS Regression Results
Dep. Variable: Sales R-squared: 0.239
Model: OLS Adj. R-squared: 0.235
Method: Least Squares F-statistic: 62.43
Date: Sun, 21 Jun 2026 Prob (F-statistic): 2.66e-24
Time: 14:59:34 Log-Likelihood: -927.66
No. Observations: 400 AIC: 1861.
Df Residuals: 397 BIC: 1873.
Df Model: 2
Covariance Type: nonrobust
coef std err t P>|t| [0.025 0.975]
Intercept 13.0308 0.631 20.652 0.000 11.790 14.271
US[T.Yes] 1.1996 0.258 4.641 0.000 0.692 1.708
Price -0.0545 0.005 -10.416 0.000 -0.065 -0.044
Omnibus: 0.666 Durbin-Watson: 1.912
Prob(Omnibus): 0.717 Jarque-Bera (JB): 0.749
Skew: 0.092 Prob(JB): 0.688
Kurtosis: 2.895 Cond. No. 607.


Notes:
[1] Standard Errors assume that the covariance matrix of the errors is correctly specified.

After removing the non-significant predictor Urban, the smaller model includes only Price and US. The fitted model is:

Sales=13.0308−0.0545(Price)+1.1996(USYes)

Both Price and US remain statistically significant because their p-values are below 0.05.

(f)¶

The original model and the smaller model have almost the same R-squared value of 0.239. This means both models explain about 23.9% of the variation in Sales. Since removing Urban did not reduce the R-squared value, the smaller model fits about as well as the original model.

In [19]:
#(g)
model_10_small.conf_int()
Out[19]:
0 1
Intercept 11.79032 14.271265
US[T.Yes] 0.69152 1.707766
Price -0.06476 -0.044195

The 95% confidence interval for the intercept is approximately 11.79 to 14.27. The 95% confidence interval for US[T.Yes] is approximately 0.69 to 1.71. The 95% confidence interval for Price is approximately -0.065 to -0.044.

Since the confidence intervals for US[T.Yes] and Price do not include 0, this supports the conclusion that both predictors are statistically significant. US has a positive relationship with Sales, while Price has a negative relationship with Sales.

In [20]:
#(h)
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

influence_10 = model_10_small.get_influence()

leverage_10 = influence_10.hat_matrix_diag
student_resid_10 = influence_10.resid_studentized_internal

plt.scatter(leverage_10, student_resid_10)
plt.axhline(0)
plt.xlabel("Leverage")
plt.ylabel("Studentized residuals")
plt.title("Studentized Residuals vs Leverage")
plt.show()

There do not appear to be extreme outliers because the studentized residuals are mostly between -3 and 3. There are a few higher-leverage points, but they do not appear extreme.

12. This problem involves simple linear regression without an intercept¶

(a)¶

The coefficient estimates will be the same when the sum of the squared values of X equals the sum of the squared values of Y.

In [21]:
#(b)

rng = np.random.default_rng(1)

x = rng.normal(size=100)
y = 2 * x + rng.normal(size=100)

model_y_on_x = sm.OLS(y, x).fit()
model_x_on_y = sm.OLS(x, y).fit()

print("Y on X coefficient:", model_y_on_x.params[0])
print("X on Y coefficient:", model_x_on_y.params[0])

Y on X coefficient: 1.9762423774420503
X on Y coefficient: 0.37574368103348876

In this example, the coefficient estimate for the regression of Y onto X is approximately 1.9762, while the coefficient estimate for the regression of X onto Y is approximately 0.3757. Since these values are different, this example shows that the two regressions do not generally produce the same coefficient estimate when there is no intercept.

In [22]:
#(c)
rng = np.random.default_rng(2)

x = rng.normal(size=100)
y = x.copy()

model_y_on_x_same = sm.OLS(y, x).fit()
model_x_on_y_same = sm.OLS(x, y).fit()

print("Y on X coefficient:", model_y_on_x_same.params[0])
print("X on Y coefficient:", model_x_on_y_same.params[0])

Y on X coefficient: 1.0000000000000002
X on Y coefficient: 1.0000000000000002

In this example, the coefficient estimate for the regression of Y onto X is approximately 1.0000, and the coefficient estimate for the regression of X onto Y is also approximately 1.0000. The coefficients are the same because X and Y are identical in this example.
